title: Novel Nanocarbons—Structure, Properties, and Potential Applications
abstract:
Novel NanocarbonsÐStructure, Properties,
and Potential Applications
By Shekhar Subramoney*
The 1985 discovery of the third form of ordered carbon, commonly known as the fullerenes, spurred the subsequent discovery of a
number of related novel forms of carbon. The most famous among these carbons are the nanotubes, sometimes referred to as
buckytubes as well. In thisreview, Idiscuss the structure,properties,and potential applications of most ofthese novel carbons.It is
also interesting to examine how the various forms of nanocarbons are related to one another, and the conditions under which one
form transforms to another. From the vast amount of research that has been carried out over the last decade, it is apparent that
some of these nanocarbons would have extremely unique properties that might be of value for specific applications that are being
contemplated by various researchers. The real breakthrough for this technology would be new processing methods to produce
such novel structures in commercial quantities.
1. Introduction
In historic perspective, the serendipitous discovery of the
buckminsterfullerene molecule in 1985[1] is unique as it is
perhaps the first new allotrope of any element to have been
discovered in the 20th century. The fullerene molecules can
be considered as the third form of ordered carbon after
diamond and graphite, whose structures have been well
documented for decades.[2] The most famous and most stable
of the fullerene molecules is C60, whose computer simulated
images, which are very similar in appearance to the soccer
ball, have become commonplace in a number of scientific
publications and textbooks. The C60 molecule consists of 20
hexagonal and 12 disjoint pentagonal faces, with a carbon
atom at each corner of the individual polygons. The stability
of C60 has been attributed to the ªpentagon ruleº and the
satisfaction of all valences when the pentagonal faces that
lead to the closure of individual molecules are disjointed.[3,4]
The ideal diamond structure consists of carbon atoms that
are bonded to their nearest neighbors by strong covalent
tetragonal sp3-bonding hybridization, whereas the ideal
graphite structure consists of layers of hexagonal arrays of
carbon in an ABAB-planar stacking arrangement. Although
the layer planes themselves are bonded to one another by
weak van der Waals forces, individual atoms in each layer are
bonded to the three nearest neighbors by strong covalent
trigonal sp2 bonds. In the C60 molecule, the bonding between
adjacent carbon atoms is nominally sp2, although the
curvature of the trigonal bonds leads to a component of
ªpseudoº sp3 bonding in the structure as well.
The utilization of the arc-discharge process for the
vaporization of graphite electrodes demonstrated that it
was possible to produce fullerene molecules[5] and a large
number of other closed hollow carbon structures in macro-
scopic quantities. These discoveries opened the door to an
avalanche of research across the world on fullerenes and
related structures. From a fundamental point of view, the
discovery of these novel nanostructures offered the research
community a unique insight into how carbon atoms bond
with other carbon atoms, how carbon reacts with other
elements under specific conditions of temperature and
pressure, and how carbon atoms can be transposed under
specific reaction conditions to form various allotropes.
This article reviews the current state of understanding of a
number of novel carbon-based nanoparticles, related in one
way or another to the fullerene molecule. This list consists of
the multiwalled and single-walled carbon nanotubes, carbon-
encapsulatedmetalnanoparticles,fullereneblackorsoot,and
carbon onions. It is also interesting to explore the interrela-
tionshipsbetweenthevariousnanostructuresofcarbonaswell
astheirrelationshipswithtraditionalformsoforderedcarbon
such as diamond and graphite. Modern materials research is
quiteincompleteifthefieldofapplicationsforthesematerials
is not explored; potential and existing applications for a
number of these materials will be discussed as well in this
review. While the arc-discharge process is currently the most
viable method to produce macroscopic amounts of fullerenes
such as C60 and C70 for research purposes, it is certainly not a
commercially feasible approach to produce materials such as
carbon nanotubes in large quantities. With this view in mind,
alternate methods to produce materials such as carbon
nanotubes and carbon-encapsulated metal nanoparticles will
be reviewed in detail as well.
2. Carbon Nanotubes
2.1. Multiwalled Tubes
Carbon filaments have been found, most often by accident,
inside furnaces containing hydrocarbon gases or carbon

monoxide, as reported by several authors as far back as
50 years ago.[6±8] These filaments, which are typically several
nanometers in diameter and several micrometers long, have
a tubular microstructure and are conventionally referred to
as vapor-grown carbon fibers (VGCFs). Recently, the VGCF
process has also been examined as a viable route to produce
high modulus carbon fibers at relatively low tempera-
tures.[9,10] The formation of these filaments has been
attributed to the catalytic action of several metals (or their
oxides or carbides), as documented in some well-written
review articles.[11,12] A number of authors have extensively
studied the formation of carbonaceous filaments by the
decomposition of specific groups of carbon-containing gases
and the reader is referred to these works for relevant
information.[13±18]
It is believed that filamentous carbon formation is
catalyzed by small metal particles as well as inhomogeneities
on the reactor walls of the furnaces. The commonly accepted
mechanism for the formation of these filaments is the
decomposition of the carbon-containing gas on one side of
the metal, the dissolution and diffusion of the carbon atoms
from the carbon-containing gas through the metal particle,
and precipitation of carbon on the other side of the metal
particle to form a hollow-cored filament. In a recent analysis
of the diffusion process involved in the formation of these
hollow-cored filaments,[19] it was summarized that the rate of
carbon deposition is controlled largely by the isothermal
diffusion of carbon through the metal particle.
The predecessor to what we refer to as a carbon nanotube
was discovered in the late 1950s.[20] It is referred to as a
graphite whisker and it was grown in a DC arc in an argon
atmosphere under high pressure and temperature condi-
tions. A schematic representation of a scroll-type graphite
whisker is shown in Figure 1. What set the graphite whiskers
apart from any known forms of carbon filaments were their
physical properties, which could be measured and deter-
mined to be close to ªidealº graphite values. Based on
structural and mechanical property analyses, it was deter-
mined that the graphite whiskers consisted of one or more
concentric tubes, with each tube being in the form of a scroll
extending continuously along the length of the filament, with
the graphitic c-axis being exactly normal to the filament axis.
These whiskers were found to be very strong and flexible,
with tensile strengths and modulus values of approximately
20 GPa and 1 TPa, respectively.
Carbon nanotubes or buckytubes, which can be considered
as a derivative of both the carbon fibers and fullerenes, are
processed using conditions identical to the fullerene-making
process (the Krätschmer±Huffman process)[5] and were
discovered serendipitously in 1991.[21] Microstructural anal-
ysis of theses nanotubes indicates that they are a seamless
array of cylindrical sheets of graphene with aspect ratios
typically greater than 100 and with outer diameters mostly in
the tens of nanometers, so that they could essentially be
considered as single-dimensional objects. Unlike the con-
ventional fullerenes such as C60 and C70, which are found to
be mixed with the soot produced in the vapor phase of the
arc-discharge process, the multiwalled tubes occur in the
ªbouleº that grows on the face of the negative electrode
(cathode), along with other nanoparticles such as polyhedra,
graphitic sheets, and amorphous carbon. Electron diffraction
analysis indicated that each carbon nanotube consists of a
hexagonal array of carbon atoms arranged in a helical
fashion around the tube axis, and that the pitch varies for
each of the concentric tubes within an individual multiwalled
tube. Subsequent studies clearly indicated a direct correla-
tion between the concentration of carbon nanotubes
produced and the pressure of the inert gas within the arc-
discharge chamber.[22]
S. Subramoney/Novel Nanocarbons
Shekhar Subramoney received his Bachelor's degree in Metallurgy from the Indian Institute of
Technology, Madras, India, in 1981. He obtained his Ph.D. in Materials Science from Michigan
State University in 1987, studying the age-hardening phenomenon in spinodally decomposed
copper±nickel based alloys. After two years of post-doctoral work at the University of Arizona,
where he studied novel materials such as polymeric molecular composites and sol-gel synthesized
ceramics, he joined the Fibers Research Division of the DuPont Company in Delaware, USA, in
1989. He presently works as anelectron microscopist with the Central Researchand Development
division of the DuPont Company. His current research interests include the structure and
properties of carbon-based catalysts and novel forms of nanocarbons.

from [20] with kind permission from the American Institute of Physics.

As shown in Figure 2, the arc-discharge processed bucky-
tubes are relatively defect-free compared to the VGCFs. The
discovery of these novel single-dimensional nanostructures
prompted several theoretical studies on their potential
mechanical and electrical properties.[23±31] Because of the
simplicity of structure of the single-walled nanotubes, the
theoretical approaches were mostly directed towards the
single-walled (with the additional requirements that they be
cylindrical in shape and capped at both ends by hemispher-
ical fullerene-like caps) rather than the multiwalled tubes,
although some of the studies considered multiwalled tubes
with a limited number of nested tubes.[30,31] However, several
researchers were also interested in characterizing the rather
intricate structural details of thicker multiwalled tubes,
mostly through the use of high spatial resolution techniques
such as nanoprobe electron diffraction and high-resolution
transmission electron microscopy (TEM).[21,32±37] While
Iijima[21] proposed that individual multiwalled tubes are
composed of concentric single-walled tubes of different
helicities arranged in a Russian-doll pattern, some out-
standing electron microscopy of cross-sectioned buckytubes
by Dravid et al.[33] clearly demonstrates that some of the
tubes have a scroll-like structure, with termination of the
layer plane forming the scroll clearly visible in the core of the
tube. Other authors[34±36] have also demonstrated that the
stacking of the graphene layer planes within individual tubes
is faulted to such an extent that graphitic and turbostratic
ordering of the layer planes exist side by side, and some of the
thicker tubes tend to become polygonized.[37]
An alternative approach to the processing of multiwalled
carbon nanotubes is by the pyrolysis of hydrocarbons such as
benzene[38] at about 1000 C. Although as pyrolyzed carbon
nanotubes are fairly defect-laden, similar to the VGCFs, high
temperature heat treatment in inert atmospheres renders
their structures comparable to the arc-discharge processed
carbon nanotubes. Interestingly enough, pyrolyzed carbon
nanotubes are often found on the fracture surfaces of
Considering the increasing level of understanding that was
developing on a theoretical basis of these novel structures, it
became very apparent to the research community that single-
walled tubes would be significantly more exciting than their
multiwalled counterparts. These thoughts were well cap-
tured by Dresselhaus in a Nature ªNews and Viewsº article as
early as 1992.[40] While several investigators had observed
single-walled tubes terminating within the confines of nested
multiwalled tubes in high-resolution TEM micrographs, it
was only in 1993 that macroscopic quantities of very uniform
single-walled tubes were synthesized almost simultaneously
by different groups at NEC and IBM.[41,42] Figure 3 is a TEM
image of bundles of single-walled tubes formed in the gas
phase of an arc-discharge experiment when Co was

The striking difference of the single-walled tubes formed
by the co-evaporation of carbon and a transition metal
compared to the multiwalled tubes formed in the boule of the
cathode is the uniformity of size of the former and their
relative lack of any defects that could potentially affect any
of their properties. It was immediately inferred by the
research community that if these tubes could be effectively
separated from the amorphous carbon and the nanometer-
sized metal particles that catalyzed their growth, it would be
possible to test their properties against theoretical predic-
tions. Subsequent experiments showed that the processing of
single-walled tubes could be substantially enhanced by using
combinations of rather than single transition metals to co-
evaporate with carbon.[43] While several investigators
examined the microstructure of these and other variations
of single-walled tubes such as what are humorously referred
to as ªsea urchinsº in great detail,[44±48] the biggest break-
through in single-walled nanotubetechnology came about by
reverting to the original technique of synthesizing conven-
tional fullerenesÐthe use of the laser vaporization tech-
nique. In quick succession, papers from the Rice University
Center for Nanoscale Science and Technology[49,50] clearly
demonstrated that co-evaporation of carbon with mixtures
of small concentrations of transition metals using laser
beams as the energy source was extremely successful in
producing large quantities of single-walled tubes with
relatively small concentrations of side products such as
X-ray diffraction (XRD) and electron microscopy analysis
of the materials produced at Rice University using the laser-
vaporization technique showed that the single-walled tubes
self-organized into fairly thick ropes, each of which consisted
of several hundred single-walled tubes. Figure 4 is a
remarkable electron micrograph of one of these ropes in
end-on perspective. Single-walled nanotubes, based on
theoretical calculations, are expected to have remarkable
mechanical properties, as well as electronic and magnetic
properties that could theoretically be controlled by varying
the diameter and the chirality of the individual tubes. Based
on data recently generated by the research group at Rice
University, single-walled nanotube yields as high as 90 %
have been achieved. Electrical measurements indicate that
these ªropesº of single-walled tubes are metallic, with single
rope resistivities lower than 10±4 W cm at room temperature.
In terms of uniformity, it is believed that most of the tubes in
the ropes are of the arm-chair configuration, with a (10,10)
conformation. Figure 5 is a schematic representation of
some of the possible different conformations into which a
single-walled carbon nanotube could organize itself into.[51]
However, a recent analysis of the structure of these ropes by
electron nanodiffraction[52] appears to indicate that the
individual single-walled tubes constituting a rope have a
uniform distribution of helicities, ranging from the zig-zag to
3. Properties and Applications of Carbon
One of the buzz words in materials science in the 1980s was
molecular composites; materials where the reinforcement
was smaller than the Griffith crack size and materials where

American Association for the Advancement of Science.

Following Iijima's discovery in 1991,[21] the strong notion in
the research community was that carbon nanotubes would be
ideal molecular-level composite reinforcements[40,53] in
polymeric, ceramic, or metal matrices due to their light
weight, potentially outstanding mechanical properties, and
excellent thermal conductivity in the axial direction. How-
ever, these notions werebased on the assumption that carbon
nanotubes could be produced in large quantities and isolated
from the rest of the products (such as graphite sheets and
amorphous carbon) from the arc-discharge experiment. On a
commercial basis, it was also extremely apparent that a
discontinuous process such as the arc would not be a feasible
technique to mass produce multiwalled carbon nanotubes.
However, from the point of view of the research community,
the multiwalled carbon nanotubes were ideal candidates for
the examination of several properties.
Several unique aspects of the structure of carbon
nanotubes gave sufficient reason to cause significant excite-
ment among materials scientists, physicists, and chemists the
world over when they were discovered in 1991. The
nanotubes were helical, cylindrical, relatively defect free,
highly conductive due to their graphitic structure, and
possibly the strongest fiber ever to exist with an extremely
attractive stiffness to weight ratio. The extremely narrow
inner core of the multiwalled carbon nanotubes, sometimes
as small as 2 nm, could potentially make them quantum
pipes. However, their nanometer-scale size was and remains
a serious challenge to the experimental measurement of any
Measurement of the electronic properties of individual
single-walled nanotubes to corroborate the theoretical
predictions have not been achieved to date. However, a
limited amount of experimental data on the electronic
properties of carbon nanotubes has been generated using a
combination of scanning tunneling microscopy and spec-
troscopy (STM and STS) to access single multiwalled tubes
in a bundle containing several tubes.[54,55] The experiments
involved a rather sophisticated technique of submicrometer
lithographic patterning of gold films with an STM to attach
electrical contacts to single microbundles of carbon nano-
tubes. These experiments demonstrated that multiwalled
carbon nanotubes behave similar to nanowires, with a
density of states and an energy gap dependence inversely
proportional to the tube diameter. Based on the measure-
ment of electrical resistance as a function of temperature, it
appears that multiwalled carbon nanotubes are semimetallic
and behave similar to rolled-up sheets of graphene. How-
ever, measurements below 1 K were found to be extremely
puzzling to the investigators,[55] and remain unexplained to
date. The major challenge for future experimentalists would
be to attach four nanoprobes to a single nanotube, which
would allow direct measurement of the transport properties
and enable a more precise correlation of the electronic
properties to the tube diameter and chirality.
Similar to carbon fibers, the most important properties
that would determine the nature of the potential applications
of carbon nanotubes are their mechanical properties. While
micrometer-sized carbon fibers (such as the common
polyacrylonitrile- (PAN-) based or mesophase pitch-based
fibers) are relatively easy to handle for the measurement of
mechanical properties such as tenacity, modulus (or stiff-
ness), and elongation, the situation is quite the converse with
carbon nanotubes. On the other hand, the nanometer-scale
diameters of carbon nanotubes permit very easy visualiza-
tion of their intricate structural details by high-resolution
TEM, and fairly accurate predictions can be made regarding
their mechanical properties based on what is visualized. For
example, it was extremely apparent to the research commu-
nity, from the moment that Iijima's original micrographs of
carbon nanotubes were published,[21] that these would be the
ultimate fibers, with exceptional stiffness and strength in the
axial direction. Similarly, based on a single high-resolution
image, Ruoff et al.[56] have shown that weak van der Waals
forces between adjacent tubes are sufficient to deform
carbon nanotubes in the lateral direction and destroy the
cylindrical symmetry of the tubes. This finding predicts that
the physical properties of carbon nanotubes could be altered
by their surroundings. When single-walled carbon nanotubes
were discovered in 1993,[41,42] it was very apparent that they
were relatively defect-free compared to their multiwalled
counterparts, and more flexible.
To date, most of the experimental work to study the
mechanical properties of carbon nanotubes has been
confined to very empirical experiments involving multi-
walled carbon nanotubes. For example, carbon nanotubes
were placed on TEM grids coated with a heat-sensitive
material such as Formvar, which can be deformed either in
situ in the TEM by a high electron beam flux or ex situ using a
heat source such a soldering gun held several centimeters
away from the grid.[57,58] Based on high-resolution images of
the deformed nanotubes, as shown in Figure 6, it can be
inferred that the tubes are under a fairly complex state of

sional side of the carbon nanotube clearly suffers more
damage than the tensile side, and it is believed that shear
stresses that develop between the planes when the nanotube
is bent ultimately leads to rupture of individual graphene
layers by the Reynolds±Sharp mechanism.[59] The deforma-
tion and failure observed so easily by TEM in carbon
nanotubes could also offer significant insights into the more
complex deformation and failure that occur in more
macroscopic systems such as carbon fibers or rigid-rod
polymeric fibers used in advanced composite applications.
Recently, the Young's modulus of multiwalled carbon
nanotubes was also estimated based on the amplitude of
intrinsic thermal vibrations in the TEM,[60] which appears to
indicate that carbon nanotubes have exceptionally high
stiffness in the axial direction (in the TPa range).
Based on what the reader sees in the several hundreds of
published micrographs of carbon nanotubes, it is difficult to
not draw similarities between the nanotube and a drinking
straw. Several researchers were excited about the possibility
of opening the ends of carbon nanotubes and subsequently
filling them with a foreign material to produce exotic
materials such as metallic nanowires. The methods typically
involved the oxidation of the ends of the carbon nanotubes
using agents such as O2 or CO2,[61±65] followed by the
annealing of the product with a low-melting metal or oxide.
In all of these experiments it was demonstrated that the ends
of the carbon nanotubes are preferentially oxidized due to
the relatively larger concentration of pentagons at those
sites, followed by the filling of the carbon nanotube by the
metal or oxide for several nanometers from the opened end.
However, these experiments did not develop into anything
more substantial, such as completely filled nanotubes or
demonstrations that one could process macroscopic quan-
tities of metallic nanowires by this route. Another obvious
inference from all of the micrographs that the reader sees is
the extraneous material produced in association with the
carbon nanotubes, such as amorphous carbon, whether they
be multiwalled or single-walled. A number of investigators
have examined the possibility of purifying both types of
nanotubes.[66,67] However, the major drawback of all these
experiments is the relatively poor yield of nanotubes
obtained following purification. A fair measure of success
in obtaining fairly pure yields of multiwalled carbon
nanotubes was recently reported.[68] This approach involved
a liquid-phase separation of multiwalled nanotubes and
nanoparticles, and this could signify a very important
breakthrough for a number of nanoparticle-related technol-
One of the most common questions raised by researchers
in the field of carbon nanotubes concerns the possible
relationship between single and multiwalled tubes, both
products of the arc-discharge process. While single-walled
tube ends are seen frequently in nested multiwalled tubes, it
became very apparent to the research community that there
were substantial differences between the formation pro-
cesses for single- and multiwalled tubes. Single-walled tubes
are never observed in the growth on the face of the cathode,
and vice versa, multiwalled tubes are never observed in the
gas-phase product following arc-discharge experiments
where transition metals or mixtures of transition metals
were incorporated into the anode. However, high-tempera-
ture annealing of the material containing significant quan-
tities of single-walled tubes results in the formation of
multiwalled tubes with two to three layer wall thicknesses.[69]
This implies that the single-walled tubes serve as substrates
for the epitaxial growth of multiwalled tubes. Figure 7 is a
high-resolution TEM image illustrating the microstructure
obtained following high-temperature annealing of a Ni-
catalyzed soot containing a large number of single-walled
tubes. It is interesting to observe how the multiwalled tubes
formed as a result of the annealing process are segmented,
and that the maximum wall thickness does not exceed three
or four graphene layer planes.

To date, carbon nanotubes have not been utilized for any
commercial applications. However, based on their unique
structure and properties, several investigators have either
proposed or experimented with potential applications that
may ultimately become commercial reality. These applica-
tions range from quantum nanowires[70] to supports for metal
particles in catalysis[71] to electron field emitters[72] to
nanoscale storage devices for gases such as hydrogen for
fuel-cell electric vehicles.[73] Of these, the last two possibil-
ities appear very exciting. In order to use carbon nanotubes
as field-emitting tips, it is necessary to be able to process
them in film form, with the individual nanotubes arranged in
ordered arrays normal to the film plane. As will be explained
later in this review, one of the current trends in the field of
nanocarbons is the processing of nanotubular arrays using
different techniques. Some of the latest developments[74]
successfully demonstrate the formation of nanotubes in such
arrays with essentially no extraneous material produced. We

4. Carbon Nanopolyhedra and Carbon-
Encapsulated Metal Nanoparticles
The other interesting nanostructure that was discovered as
a by-product of multiwalled carbon nanotubes was the
multiwalled polyhedron. Figure 8 is a high-resolution TEM
image depicting typical polyhedral nanoparticles consisting
of a regular faceted shape enclosing a void in the middle. It is
apparent that each polyhedron consists of twelve pentagons
(as dictated by the Euler's theorem) each arranged at the
intersection of the faceted planes. Analogous to multiwalled
nanotubes, the nanopolyhedra are also found in the growth
that occurs on the face of the cathode. Saito et al.[75] proposed
one of the earliest theories explaining the formation
mechanism of nanopolyhedra and nanotubes. Their model
is schematically represented in Figure 9. As with carbon
nanotubes, one of the most exciting aspects of the nano-
polyhedra was the empty cavity in the middle, offering the
potential to be filled with nanoparticles with exciting
properties, while at the same time protecting these nanopar-
ticles from the influences of the atmosphere. This was
contemplated to be analogous to endohedral doping of
[76]Ðwhich involves the addition of an
atom or an ion into the core of the fullerene molecule to form
an endohedrally doped molecular unit. In fullerene parlance
these molecules are referred to as metallofullerenes or
endofullerenes. Mass spectrometry of the material produced
following laser evaporation of a graphitic target doped with
lanthanum chloride in water suggested that the C60
molecules were endohedrally doped with single ions of
La+. This finding supported the initial hypothesis that
molecules such as C60 had closed cage structures.[77] So it
was but natural for the initial experiments to be run with the
lanthanide metal stuffed anodes but at conditions necessary
for the formation of nanotubes and nanopolyhedra.[22]
TEM of the material obtained from the core of the growth
on the cathode, following our experiments with graphitic
anodes incorporating a small percentage of La2O3,[78]
revealed that a sizable fraction of the product consisted of
carbon-encapsulated metal nanoparticles, which have since
then been abbreviated as CEMNs. Figure 10 is a high-
resolution TEM image of such a CEMN. In reality, the
encapsulates were mostly MC2, where M is a lanthanide
metal, as determined by lattice spacings and electron
diffraction. Most of the encapsulates were single crystals as
well, as evidenced by the lack of grain boundaries in the high-
resolution images, and the crystal boundaries conformed to
the interior carbon shell walls, suggesting that the particle
was completely enclosed within the carbon shell. This was
also confirmed by the fact that the lanthanide carbides did
not degrade after exposure to air for considerable lengths of
time; lanthanide carbides are hygroscopic and are known to
readily hydrolyze in air. Although the first metal to be
encapsulated in the form of a CEMN was La,[78,79] it was
subsequently demonstrated that the other rare earth metals

From the list of applications stated in the previous
paragraph, it became very apparent that the key issue of
interest for the CEMNs would be their magnetic properties.
A number of researchers have devoted a considerable
amount of their efforts to evaluating the magnetic properties
of a variety of CEMNs; this is critical due to the strong
dependence of several technical magnetic properties, most
notably the coercivity, with the particle size of the material
under consideration. This was because the coercivity of
several ferromagnets was strongly dependent on the size of
the monodomains, with maximum values of coercivities
being obtained at monodomain sizes of about 100 nm. Some
outstanding work on carbon-encapsulated fine particle
ferromagnets has been done by McHenry et al. from the
Carnegie±Mellon University[82,83] over the last three to four
years. They have clearly demonstrated that when transition
metals such as Co and Fe are encapsulated into carbon shells
as nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm they have magnetic
coercivities comparable to iron oxide nanoparticles synthe-
sized by a matrix-mediated route. They have also shown that
the protective carbon coating ensures that the metal or
carbide encapsulate is retained in the reduced state. This
prevents the ferromagnetic CEMN from exhibiting ex-
change bias due to interfacial reactions between the
ferromagnetic metal and the antiferromagnetic oxide coat-
ings that normally develop on magnetic metal particles.
Some of the latest work by this group has shown that carbon-
encapsulated alloy magnetic particles could be synthesized
as well by the arc-discharge process; it is expected that alloy
magnets such as SmxCoy with large magnetocrystalline
anisotropies would provide potentially thermally stable fine
particle magnets. Carbon-encapsulated soft ferromagnets
(such as FexCoy) with high Curie temperatures have also

combination of the attributes of carbon-encapsulated mag-
netic metal nanoparticles. For example, the superparamag-
netic response of carbon-encapsulated GdC2 nanocrystals,
their nanometer scale dimensions (typically 20±50 nm), and
the highly adsorbent carbon coating made these CEMNs
ideal candidates for the delivery of biological substances into

The conventional arc process (the Krätschmer±Huffman
process) was clearly not the ideal method to produce large
quantities of any type of CEMNs. This reality prompted
research into alternate means of producing carbon-coated
metal nanoparticles. The first major breakthrough was
reported in 1995 by the group from Northwestern University,
with what they termed as the blown-arc process.[85] In this
work, the authors demonstrated that extremely fine nano-
particles (typically smaller than 10 nm diameter) could be
produced in fairly pure form by an arc process where a
helium stream directed at the arc between a Ni anode and a
W cathode reduced the Ni vapor concentration and
increased the quench rate. They also showed that the helium
gas velocity was the major factor in controlling the particle
size; a large fraction of the Ni particles produced with a gas
velocity of 56 m/s were under 10 nm in diameter. A
modification of this process with a graphite crucible holding
the anode disk (such as a Ni disk) resulted in the formation of
carbon-encapsulated metal nanoparticles with a small
concentration of carbonaceous debris and bare metal
nanoparticles, which were easily removed using acid
baths.[86] Figure 11 is a high-resolution TEM image of
CEMNs (encapsulating single crystals of Ni) produced by
the blown-arc process. Some striking morphological differ-
ences with respect to the conventional CEMNs processed by
the Krätschmer±Huffman process become immediately
apparent; the CEMNs processed by the blown-arc method
are more or less spherical, the carbon layers encapsulating
them are less well ordered and more defective, and the
carbon shells do not typically enclose a void adjacent to the
metal encapsulate. This last observation would imply that the
CEMNs processed by the blown-arc method are denser than
the CEMNs processed by the conventional arc process, and,
considering that ferromagnetic crystals such as Co, Fe, and Ni
could be easily encapsulated by the blown-arc method, they

One of the more interesting facets that developed from all
of the work on nanocarbons was the multitude of novel
shapes observed under different processing conditions, and
how the investigators responsible for their discovery were
quick enough to jump on the bandwagon with catchy titles
for their individual discoveries. In fact, some of these findings
were aptly summarized in a cartoon that appeared in an issue
of the MRS Bulletin dedicated to fullerenes and related
materials,[87] and this cartoon is illustrated in Figure 12. At
present, it is fairly apparent that the structures depicted in
Figure 12 may not have any significant commercial or
specific scientific value, yet it is extremely intriguing to note
the effect that different metals or processing conditions have
on the microstructure of carbon. Structures such as sea
urchins (catalyzed by Gd),[88] bamboo trunks, and necklaces
(catalyzed by Ni)[89,90] are some of the more fascinating
microstructures to emerge from the interaction of carbon
and metals in the vapor phase. While these structures were
reported as soon as they were discovered, there is as yet no
consensus on how these novel nanostructures form within
the confines of the arc-discharge chamber. On a parallel
note, it is also worthwhile mentioning that structural
variations to carbon nanotubes, such as cones,[91] have also
been observed by other investigatorsÐthese features were
formed without the aid of catalyzing agents such as a metal.

appears exactly the same as any common carbon black, is
quite an extraordinary material warranting further research
into its properties and potential applications. To start with,
besides the fairly high concentration of C60 (along with a

typically found to contain a variety of higher fullerenes such
as C76, C84, C92Ðby a number of workers. Typical C60/C70

The XRD characteristics of fullerene blacks have also
been shown to be different from conventional carbon blacks
with the presence of faint peaks at small scattering angles,[94]
and this may be attributable to small diffraction units of
microporous material present in fullerene blacks. Oxygen-
bomb combustion calorimetry of fullerene blacks indicates
that the standard energy of combustion is closer to that of
fullerenes such as C60 and C70 than to that of graphite,[95]
which clearly demonstrates that the structure of fullerene
blacks might be more akin to the conventional fullerenes
than any other carbon blacks. While it is quite evident that
fullerene blacks might have unique structures and properties
compared to usual carbon blacks, we, as a research
community, are not yet completely certain what these
differences signify, or what might be the potential applica-
tions of these novel materials.
Considering the several similarities between conventional
fullerenes and fullerene blacks, it was not very surprising
when it was convincingly demonstrated that fullerene blacks
converted readily to fullerene-like carbon onion particles
upon irradiation with strong doses of electron beams in an
electron microscope.[96] It was subsequently demonstrated
that fullerene blacks convert to carbon onions significantly
faster than the usual carbon blacks.[93] These studies
suggested that planar graphite might not be the energetically
most stable allotrope of carbon, and initiated several
research efforts at establishing the interrelationships be-
tween the various allotropes of carbon.

of different treatment conditions. Subsequent to the electron
irradiation experiments, de Heer and Ugarte demonstrated

Figure 15 aptly indicate the microstructural changes to the

converted to similar-sized carbon onions by thermal trans-
formation. Figures 16a and 16b are high-resolution TEM
images of the as-produced nanodiamonds and the nano-
onions formed from the diamonds as a result of thermal

either thermal treatment or electron beam bombardment[100]

apparent that the diamond to graphitic onion-like structure
is produced by atom displacement events, such as knock-on

atoms from their original lattice sites followed by transporta-

On a reverse note, it was recently demonstrated that the
cores of carbon onions act as nanoscopic pressure cells and
provide sites for the local formation of nanometer-scale
diamond crystals,[101] as shown in Figure 17. In this work,
electron bombardment of the carbon onion particles formed
from graphitic particles (as explained in the literature[97])
resulted in the formation of nanodiamonds, possibly due to a
combination of the high temperature due to the high-voltage
electron beam (over 700 C at the specimen stage) and
pressure induced by the structure itself at the center.
In summary, it is apparent that the different forms of
nanocarbons are intimately related to one another. Under
different sets of processing conditions, we have observed that
graphite can transform to fullerenes, fullerene black or soot,
and multiwalled carbon nanotubes and nanopolyhedra.
Doping of graphite with different metals or oxides results
in the formation of exotic structures such as carbon-
encapsulated metal nanoparticles and single-walled tubes.

7. Current Trends in and the Future of Carbon
Over the last five to six years since Iijima's discovery of
multiwalled carbon nanotubes,[21] a number of novel
nanostructures of carbon and associated materials have
been discovered and reported, as described in this review. It
is fairly apparent that we are at the stage of research where
issues such as unique properties and potentially unique
applications of these novel class of materials are becoming
more important than the finding of yet other structures that
may be classified as nanocarbons. On this basis, several
investigators are seriously examining alternate routes to
synthesizing nanocarbons. It is fairly evident that nanotubes,
both the single- and multiwalled variety, would be the key
materials for further research, with particular emphasis on
their processing in bulk quantities and in forms amenable to
immediate application. With these viewpoints in mind, it is
very apparent that the conventional process of synthesizing
nanotubes (by the arc-discharge process) would not be a
commercially viable process for a number of reasons.
Theuse of double laser sources[50] is clearly a manifestation
of the efforts to synthesize single-walled tubes in large
quantities. As mentioned earlier in this review, the yields
using this approach are being fine-tuned to about the 90 %
extent. Efforts will, no doubt, be directed at cleaning up these
products to obtain almost pure single-walled tubes. On a
parallel basis, research efforts will also be focused on
potentially unique applications of materials such as single-
walled tubes. As illustrated recently by Dillon et al.,[73]
single-walled tubes produced in large quantities might be
potential storage devices for hydrogen for use in fuel-cell
vehicles. If this can be demonstrated at a commercial level, it
would have tremendously positive implications for the
massive automotive industryÐparticularly with respect to
environmental considerations. While it is believed that
single-walled tubes could become potential storage devices
for condensed gases such as hydrogen by preferentially
opening their caps via oxidation and the subsequent filling of
the opened tubes by capillary forces, other workers have also
recently shown that the structures of multiwalled tubes could
be preferentially engineered to produce devices capable of
storing hydrogen almost three times their own weight under
pressure at room temperatureÐmore than ten times as much
as current storage media.[102,103] Figure 18 clearly illustrates
how the microstructure of catalytically grown carbon

orientation. As these research efforts are still in an

As a concluding remark, it is also important to note that
the research efforts in nanocarbons have spawned parallel
work on other forms of nanostructures such as boron nitride
nanotubes[105] and metal dichalcogenide nanoparticles such
as MX2 (where M is either Mo or W and X is either S or
Se).[106] As with carbon nanostructures, if methods can be
developed to process these materials in bulk quantities using
commercial processes, there would be several applica-
tions[107] where these novel materials can fill special niches.
As we progress into the 21st century, it is very likely that a
significant part of materials science will involve materials
whose properties would be assessed on the basis of their
structures, which can be determined only by sophisticated
analytical techniques. How we effectively utilize these
materials is a major challenge to scientists and engineers
